data.title: China Gold Market Update: Wholesale Demand Rebounded
data.description:
Highlights

  • Gold continues to shine. The LBMA Gold Price PM in USD and the Shanghai Benchmark Gold Price PM (SHAUPM) in RMB both soared by 12% last month, extending y-t-d gains to levels unseen for decades
  • Gold withdrawals from the Shanghai Gold Exchange (SGE) reached 118t in September, 33t higher m/m and 4t higher y/y; Q3 ended with a total withdrawal of 297t, 9t lower y/y
  • Chinese gold ETFs saw inflows in September (RMB4.5bn, US$622mn), narrowing their Q3 losses, and gold futures activity on the Shanghai Futures Exchange (SHFE) improved notably
  • The People’s Bank of China (PBoC) reported its 11th consecutive monthly gold purchase, adding 1.2t in September and pushing the Q3 total to 5t.

Looking ahead

  • After re-stocking in September, gold jewellery retailers will likely dial back their replenishment in October, following seasonal trends. Furthermore, the gold price rally may continue to weigh on gold jewellery consumption, adding to retailer caution.
  • Investment demand for gold may be buoyed by the attractive gold price rally, particularly when the local equity momentum took a hit amid flaring US-China tension.

Chart 1: The SHAUPM saw its strongest Q1~Q3 performance since its launch in 2016

Cumulative return of the SHAUPM in RMB between Q1 and Q3*

Another record-shattering month

Gold’s strength continued in September. The LBMA Gold Price PM recorded its largest monthly increase since January 2012 and its strongest quarterly performance since Q1 2016. But more notably, the SHAUPM registered its strongest month on record and its second-strongest quarter ever with a 14% gain—just behind Q1 2025’s 18% surge.

Having reached record highs 36 times during the first three quarters of 2025, the SHAUPM’s y-t-d return extended to 36%, to date the best year on record (Chart 1). The LBMA Gold Price PM jumped 43% between January and September, the best y-t-d performance since 1979. Gold’s relatively weaker return in China was mainly due to a 4% appreciation in the RMB against the dollar during the period.

It is also worth noting that the gold price has carried its bullish momentum into October, with the LBMA Gold Price PM breaching the US$4,000/oz threshold for the first time ever on 8 October and the SHAUPM surging nearly 5% during the month’s first trading day.1

Wholesale demand ended Q3 with an improving September

Wholesale gold demand saw a 33t m/m rebound in September, reaching 118t; 4t higher y/y (Chart 2). Nonetheless, it remained 33% below the long-term average. Our interactions with market participants inform us that as the gold price resumed its uptrend, bar and coin sales improved during the month, supporting wholesale demand. But some investors took the opportunity to book profits by selling their holdings.

September usually brings a seasonal uplift from manufacturers’ new product launches and retailers’ pre-Golden Week holiday restocking. Yet this year, soaring gold prices and doubts about future sales have tempered retailers' restocking enthusiasm.

Chart 2: Wholesale gold demand improved in September

Monthly gold withdrawals from the SGE*

Chart 3: Gold withdrawals from the SGE remained weak in Q3

Q3 gold withdrawals from the SGE*

Chinese gold ETFs finally saw inflows in September, following two consecutive monthly losses (Chart 4). Attracted by the strong gold price performance, Chinese investors bought RMB4.5bn (US$622mn) of local gold ETFs. September’s inflows and the surging price lifted Chinese gold ETFs’ total AUM to another month-end peak of RMB169bn (US$22bn), with holdings rebounding by 4.9t to 194t.

Chart 4: Chinese gold ETFs saw inflows in September

Monthly Chinese gold ETF flows and month-end AUM*

But September’s inflows were not sufficient to reverse earlier losses, resulting in Q3 net outflows of RMB3.8bn (US$537mn) (Chart 5) and collective holdings reduced by 5.8t during the period. As previously noted, Chinese investor risk appetite improved as the need for safe-haven assets declined: July and August saw better growth expectations on the back of strong Q2 economic data and a reducing US-China tariff risk. This sentiment was reflected in the strong CSI300 Stock Index rally, which surged 18% during Q3, the strongest quarter since Q1 2019. Meanwhile, rising government bond yields – as the market priced in fewer rate cuts by the PBoC – increased opportunity costs of holding gold.

Nonetheless, helped by various uncertainties, the strong price performance, a lack of alternative investment options, and encouragement from the central bank’s non-stop gold buying, Chinese gold ETF inflows during the first three quarters remain the largest ever. In fact, the cumulative inflows of RMB59bn (US$8.2bn, 79t) between Q1 and Q3 have surpassed all previous annual records (Chart 5).

Chart 5: Q3 outflows have little impact on y-t-d strength

Quarterly gold ETF tonnage demand*

Chart 6: Trading volumes of gold futures rebounded in September

Daily average trading volumes of SHFE gold futures and monthly gold price volatility*

The PBoC reported its 11th consecutive monthly gold purchase

In September, the PBoC announced a 1.2t gold purchase, its 11th non-stop addition. By the end of Q3 China’s official gold holdings stood at 2,303t, 7.7% of total foreign reserves.

China’s gold reserves increased by 5t, a slower pace than in previous quarters (Chart 7). This is perhaps not surprising: as the gold price surges, central banks may dial back the amount of gold buying for tactical reasons. Yet judging from the PBoC’s four-quarter non-stop gold purchases, we believe gold remains a key strategic asset to central banks looking to diversify assets in a fragmented and uncertain world.

Chart 7: Four quarters of non-stop PBoC gold purchasing announcements

Reported official gold holdings and the quarterly average LBMA Gold Price PM*

China imported a net 87t of gold in August, based on the latest information, a mild 2t m/m fall (Chart 8). Imports remained broadly stable as the average local gold price premium fell only slightly in the month (US$7.6/oz in August vs US$8/oz in July); the upcoming peak season for gold also limited the m/m weakness.

Chart 8: August gold imports remained relatively stable

Net 7108 gold imports under various regimes*

1Chinese markets were closed between 1 and 8 October due to the National Day Holiday and the Mid-Autumn Day.

data.title: 3 Steps For Active EM Bond Investing
data.description:
Emerging markets bonds offer high yields, low debt, and policy-driven resilience – this is our three-step process for capturing alpha in the space.

Emerging markets (EM) bonds have grown into one of the most compelling opportunities in fixed income. With lower debt levels, more independent central banks, and attractive real yields, many EMs are better positioned than developed markets to deliver stability and growth. Yet, EM debt remains under-owned, in part because outcomes can diverge dramatically between countries and currencies. Below, we summarize the investment process for the actively managed VanEck Emerging Markets Bond ETF, which aims to capitalize on these disparities and opportunities across the EM debt spectrum.

A Valuation-Based Investment Process

At the core of our EM bond strategy is a simple principle: buy cheap bonds, not good or bad risks. To do that, step 1 of our investment process builds a radar chart showing each country’s superiority, or inferiority, relative to the global average measured in units of standard deviation across a range of 16 fundamental metrics. These metrics include:

  • Standard measures such as general government debt/GDP
  • Flow measures such as the current account deficit/GDP
  • Structural measures, such as the banking system’s common equity-to-assets ratio

We display these as a radar chart, as shown below. When the country result is inside the global mean result, it is superior to the global mean. Conversely, when the result is outside the global mean, it is inferior to the global average.

Brazil’s Radar Chart Measuring Fundamental Risk

From there, we then “line up” all the radar charts for the countries and companies in the investable universe. We do this by calculating a z-score (a way of measuring how far a data point is from the average of a dataset) based on each country’s superiority, or inferiority, to the global mean, as measured by the metrics detailed in the chart. We compare these z-scores and bonds with similar yields. You can see the fundamental z-score on the x-axis in the chart below. Those with superior fundamentals are on the left, inferior to the right. We highlight Brazil and see that the real yields (y-axis) of Brazil’s 10-year local-currency bonds are higher than the predicted level (along the dotted line), based on all the other EM 10-year local-currency bonds. They are therefore considered cheap. We conduct this exercise for every tenor of bond. And we conduct it for hard-currency bonds using yield or spread on the y-axis, which you can see in the chart below.

In this chart, you can see that Brazilian hard-currency bonds are expensive. The output of Step 1 in our process is a list of the cheapest bonds in EM, whether local, hard, sovereign, or corporate, based entirely on their deviation from the predicted level. The cheaper they are, the more they are liked and therefore the higher they are on the list. You may notice that Step 1 favors tenors/duration based entirely on cheapness relative to fundamentals, not the investment teams’ top-down opinion on U.S. ‘duration.’ If the 20-year Brazilian local currency bond was cheaper relative to the predicted level than the 10-year Brazilian local currency bond, the 20-year bond is higher in the ranking.

Real 10-year Local Currency Valuations and Country Fundamentals Determine which 10-year Local Currency Bonds Pay Real Yields that are Relatively Too High

Sovereign 5-year Bond Valuations and Country Fundamentals Determine which 5-year Hard Currency Sovereign Bonds Pay Yields that Are Relatively Too High

The result of Step 1 is a list of the cheapest bonds in EM, without reference to whether they are local currency, hard-currency sovereign, or hard-currency corporate. It is an entirely quantitative process. In EM, however, there are many non-systematic risks, such as politics, elections, index exclusions/inclusions, global trade partnerships and treaties that are not appropriately managed by the quantitative framework in Step 1.

This is why we have Step 2, which may eliminate or impact the ranking of bonds chosen by Step 1. Step 2 of the process involves three ‘tests’:

  1. Political/policy
  2. Economic
  3. Technical A ‘strong fail’ result on any of these removes a bond from the list.

Each test is applied bond-by-bond, so the investment team can fail a 10-year local currency bond from a country but not a 5-year hard-currency bond from the same country. All changes in Step 2 are subjective and documented by the investment team monthly.

Step 3 is portfolio construction. In Step 1, the cheaper a bond is relative to the predicted level, the greater the allocation. If a bond is in the top quartile of cheapness, it gets the maximum allocation of 1.5 times the benchmark weight for that country. If a bond is in the second quartile, the maximum allocation is 1.25 times the benchmark weight for that country and so on, until the portfolio is full. The portfolio is constructed in consideration of risk constraints.

VanEck Emerging Markets Bond Strategy

The VanEck Emerging Markets Bond ETF was one of the first blended emerging markets bond strategies in the market. The Strategy adopts a comprehensive approach, investing across the entire EM bond spectrum to maximize opportunity and manage risk in a complex global environment. Despite global disruptions such as the COVID pandemic, the war in Ukraine and economic troubles in China, the fund has consistently outperformed both global and U.S. bond benchmarks, earning top-quartile rankings from Morningstar over the past five years. This outperformance, both absolute and risk-adjusted, is also evident when compared to the fund’s benchmark, which includes the same EM bonds but without active management. VanEck’s active strategy, which focuses on fundamental value relative to bond risk premia, aims to capitalize on these shifts and avoid troubled issuers, making a compelling case for a diversified, actively managed EM bond allocation.

Important Disclosures

This is not an offer to buy or sell, or a recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities, financial instruments or digital assets mentioned herein. The information presented does not involve the rendering of personalized investment, financial, legal, tax advice, or any call to action. Certain statements contained herein may constitute projections, forecasts and other forward-looking statements, which do not reflect actual results, are for illustrative purposes only, are valid as of the date of this communication, and are subject to change without notice. Actual future performance of any assets or industries mentioned are unknown. Information provided by third party sources are believed to be reliable and have not been independently verified for accuracy or completeness and cannot be guaranteed. VanEck does not guarantee the accuracy of third party data. The information herein represents the opinion of the speaker(s), but not necessarily those of VanEck or its other employees.

An investment in the VanEck Emerging Markets Bond ETF may be subject to risks which include, among others, risks related to active management, credit, credit-linked notes, currency management strategies, derivatives, emerging market issuers, ESG investing, foreign currency, foreign securities, hedging, high portfolio turnover, high yield securities, interest rate, market, non-diversified, operational, restricted securities, investing in other funds, sovereign bond, special risk considerations of investing in African, Asian, and Latin American issuers, authorized participant concentration, no guarantee of active trading market, trading issues, fund shares trading, premium/discount and liquidity of fund shares, and cash transactions risks, all of which may adversely affect the Fund. Emerging market issuers and foreign securities may be subject to securities markets, political and economic, investment and repatriation restrictions, different rules and regulations, less publicly available financial information, foreign currency and exchange rates, operational and settlement, and corporate and securities laws risks.

Investing involves substantial risk and high volatility, including possible loss of principal. An investor should consider the investment objective, risks, charges and expenses of the Funds carefully before investing. To obtain a prospectus and summary prospectus, which contain this and other information, call 800.826.2333 or visit vaneck.com. data.title: Pizza Pizza Royalty Corp.: Strategic Royalty Pool Management And Valuation Should Heat Up Upside
data.description:
Three months after my initial analysis of Pizza Pizza Royalty Corp. (OTCPK:PZRIF) (TSX:PZA:CA), we saw more developments that continued to support my buy rating. Its strategic business model and sound fundamentals ensure its sustainability and dividend longevity. Valuation remains attractive since the price has only increased by 3% since my initial coverage. Upside is still justifiable, so buying at its current level is recommended. Technicals have weakened recently but show new buying opportunities.

Before I start, I would like to note that all financials are in CAD. Meanwhile, my computations using the Dividend Discount Model are in USD.

Challenges

In my previous article, I explained that PZA's growth is deeply tied to its pool of restaurants. If their sales drop, royalties will follow. I would like to highlight that these Pizza Pizza and Pizza 73 restaurants are within the royalty pool of PZA, so they include both franchised and company-owned restaurants. Regardless of the type of restaurant, PZA's royalty only concerns itself with restaurant sales. This is to avoid confusing it with franchised restaurants.

Another challenge is that PZA does not influence or affect the decisions and strategies of Pizza Pizza Limited, or PPL, and its franchisees. It does not have a say about pricing strategies, inventory management, and cost-expense management. In short, it has limited or no control over overall business operations and management. So, it has very limited ways to grow its royalties unless it expands or acquires more licenses or royalties.

Competition should also be considered since it affects foot traffic and sales of Pizza Pizza and Pizza 73 branches. It is especially crucial in restaurants that are part of its royalty pool. Note that popular brands like Domino's (DPZ) and Pizza Hut (YUM) are also present there. Both have over 600 locations in over 300 cities. Another popular competitor is Boston Pizza (OTC:BPZZF) with nearly 400 stores in 250 cities.

Drivers/Mitigants

Despite these challenges, Pizza Pizza has some natural insulation and even growth drivers to counter these challenges.

1. PZA Enjoys A Low-Cost, Low-Capital, High-Margin Business Model. PZA does not spend on ingredients and raw materials. It also has limited labor and technology expenses. So, it is not cost-sensitive even if inflation persists.

In addition, Pizza Pizza itself remains one of the most popular pizza chains in Canada. So even if there is a high level of competition, it remains one of the key players in the group. One key aspect is its continued expansion that focuses on dense markets and late-night service, which may help it maintain its competitive edge aside from its huge domestic market presence. Aside from pricing strategies, loyalty coupons and localization strategies like regional toppings and sides ensure customer loyalty. Pizza Pizza also invests in digital platforms and partnerships with third-party apps to expand its delivery network.

2. PZA Maintains Prudent Royalty Pool Management. PZA remains very cautious about the restaurants it adds to its pool. It doesn't just acquire rights merely for its expansion. With its current revenue trajectory, I'd like to think that it carefully studies every aspect of every restaurant within its royalty pool. Location, foot traffic, market conditions, and competition level in the area are some things it has always considered before rushing into them.

It continues to pay off. As you can see, its revenue or royalty income amounted to CAD 10.3M, up by 3.8% YoY from CAD 10.0M. This is also the second quarter that it posted positive revenue growth after its soft performance from Q2 2024 to Q4 2024.

If you look at it per restaurant, PZA is generating more royalty income. In its 694 Pizza Pizza restaurants, the average royalty income is CAD 12,048 versus CAD 11,949 YoY. This means an increase of CAD 99 per restaurant. For its 100 Pizza 73 restaurants, the average royalty income is now CAD 19,830 versus CAD 18,990 YoY.

This is supported by its prudent management of the royalty pool. Note that it had a net opening of 20 locations to 794 (694 Pizza Pizza + 100 Pizza 73) versus 772 (672 Pizza Pizza + 102 Pizza 73) from the previous year. As you can see, its Pizza 73 pool decreased by two units, which could be attributed to strategic changes. In addition, PPL is still expanding while balancing it with store closures. In Q2 2025, its Pizza Pizza brand had a net opening of three units. Meanwhile, Pizza 73 remained relatively unchanged as it opened a new unit after closing one. This may increase the domestic presence of these brands. Since their number is still not that high and well-distributed, I believe that PPL can prevent cannibalization of sales.

3. PZA Promises High Profitability And Sustainability. Considering this, PZA may find new branches to add to its restaurant pool. It is possible considering its 98% operating margin. This is supported by its robust liquidity position. Its operating cash flow remains high enough to cover almost all dividends.

PZA complements it with prudent debt management. While it's high for its cash, the maturity profile remains long and decent to ensure timely repayments. In fact, no amount will mature within the year. This is in line with its net debt/EBITDA of 1.05x, which means that PZA only needs a year to repay the whole amount at once, even if it doesn't have to. Hence, it has an adequate capital buffer to sustain its operations.

4. Tamer Inflation Means Stable Spending. Inflation remains quite stubborn in Canada. Yet, it has already weakened and stabilized relative to previous years. At this point, the increase in the general price level is kept at a manageable level as inflation stays below 2.0%. On top of that, the Bank of Canada just made a 25-bps rate cut to 2.5% last month. The combination of the two may improve consumer behavior and borrowing confidence and boost economic activity. This can lead to improved labor market conditions and wages. As a result, there may be stronger purchasing power and discretionary spending in the long run. In addition, this can lead to lower borrowing costs or interest expense for PZA.

This is in line with the recent study, which shows a 12.5% increase in Canadian spending per restaurant visit. Restaurants like Pizza Pizza and Pizza 73 may also benefit from tamer inflation since 88% of Canadians consider menu price as one of their primary considerations in spending.

Valuation

To assess the stock with a forward-looking approach, I used the Dividend Discount Model. To avoid confusion, all values here are in USD. Since the quarterly payout remains the same as in my previous coverage, the annualized amount is still $0.93. For its dividend growth rate, I used Seeking Alpha's reported rate of 5.03% to make things simple. Lastly, I computed the cost of equity using the capital asset pricing model. Note that I set its risk premium at 7.0% versus the standard of 5.0% and the rate I used in my previous coverage at 6.0%. I did this to price in more risks and be more conservative. Lastly, I estimated Beta on MS Excel using the SLOPE function on the daily price change of PZA versus the S&P 500. Using all these numbers, I derived a TP of $22.95. If I add a 10% risk consideration, this will be $20.66. This proves that PZA is reasonably cheap now.

Technicals

PZA's technicals are still bullish despite its pullback in the past month. Now, it's starting to pick up its momentum as the price trend gets closer again to the 50-day SMA. The 50-day SMA is still trending upward, although a bit flattish. This reflects the recent sell-off but shows that market sentiments are starting to become optimistic again.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI warrant caution due to an early bearish divergence warning. We can see how the price set a new high from July 30 to August 26, but momentum weakened. This could mean that fewer buyers accepted any price higher than $16.38, while some even took profit. Right now, the MACD is still within negative territory, showing that selling volume still outpaces buying volume. However, it is now increasing after crossing above the signal line and getting close to positive territory, where we can see the histogram. This shows that buying volume is starting to increase again and that buyers found a key support level at $15.30, which prompted entry and/or re-entry. Lastly, RSI explains that the recent selloff was due to the overselling that prompted profit-takers to act on it. Now, momentum has normalized again after bouncing back from being nearly oversold. While it is already higher than the mid-range, it is still quite below the overbought region, so there is still enough room for buying.

Conclusion

Pizza Pizza Royalty Corp. is not fully insulated from competition and may have limited growth opportunities. But its strategic business model and prudent expansion of its royalty pool allow it to enjoy decent revenue growth, margin expansion, sustainability, and dividend longevity. This supports the cheap valuation of the stock with an attractive dividend yield and upside potential. Technicals have shown some weaknesses recently but are improving again after the slight pullback. I'm reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Managed Care Insurers Trading Down Ahead Of Earnings Season
data.description:
US managed care insurers were trading down this week ahead of earnings season as the release of Medicare ratings and federal tax credit uncertainty appeared to weigh on stock prices.

Since Oct. 8 — the day prior to the release of updated Medicare Advantage ratings — shares of nearly every major publicly traded US managed care insurer has fallen, with Humana Inc. (HUM) experiencing the sharpest decline.

The Louisville, Kentucky-based insurer was down trading at 262.17 a share as of market on Oct. 16, down 12% from Oct. 8.

During the same period, fellow insurers Centene Corp. (CNC) and The Cigna Group (CI) experienced declines of 6.9% and 4%, respectively, while Elevance Health Inc. (ELV) and UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH) experienced more modest declines of 3.5% and 2.4%, respectively.

The S&P 500 and S&P Insurance Index similarly declined, falling 1.29% and 7.03%.

While the government shutdown remains ongoing, the conversation around extending tax credits for Affordable Care Act (ACA) plans may have shifted, J.P. Morgan analysts wrote in a note.

"We believe that sentiment has improved around the possibility of an [ACA tax credit] extension as the GOP shows an increasing willingness to engage in a negotiation of some form," the analysts said.

Mixed stars ratings

Humana's sharp decline comes after the release of the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services' Medicare star ratings on Oct. 9. The government body annually issues quality ratings of Medicare Advantage offerings, which impact bonus payments insurers receive from the US government.

The insurer revealed via a Form 8-K that approximately 1.2 million of its members, or 20% of its Medicare membership, were enrolled in plans rated four stars or higher for 2026. This figure is down from 25% for 2025 and was later confirmed by a CMS release.

Humana did not respond to a request for comment but described the ratings as "generally in line with the assumptions utilized in its multiyear financial planning," in its Form 8-K.

Investors have dialed back their expectations for Humana's 2027 bonuses, which result from the ratings released in 2025, and Humana's response appears to indicate that a recovery will occur next year, the J.P. Morgan analysts said.

"Humana indicated that it remains 'confident' in its 2026 [Medicare Advantage] pricing and benefit design, which includes the assumption that the company will double individual Medicare Advantage pretax margin in 2026 (ex-Stars), and that it will return to membership growth in 2026," the analysts said.

However, Humana's decline also comes amid the dismissal of its second lawsuit against CMS over the allegedly "arbitrary and capricious actions" it committed when calculating its 2024 star ratings.

The initial lawsuit was dismissed by a federal judge in Texas for being premature, as Humana had not exhausted the administrative appeals process. The second lawsuit was effectively dismissed after the same judge granted CMS' motion for summary judgment, siding with the body's claims that it operated correctly per its procedure in lowering Humana's ratings following several disconnected and unreturned customer service calls.

"Humana agreed that if it failed as a matter of law, the record supports finding that the decision is supported," the judge wrote. "Because the no-callback policy is lawful, the agency's decision to rate the dropped calls as unsuccessful was not arbitrary and capricious."

Not all insurers saw their star ratings for 2026 decline. The overall average Medicare Advantage Star Rating for 2026 was 3.66, largely the same as 2025's average rating of 3.65, CMS data shows, and UnitedHealth and Elevance had around 78% and 55%, respectively, of their members in plans rated four stars or higher, per a Reuters report.

ACA funding

The stock price decline among managed care insurers also comes amid an ongoing government shutdown hinging on efforts by the Republican majority to do away with COVID-era tax credits for ACA plans.

The enhancements, including broadened eligibility for the ACA tax credits, expire on Dec. 31, and, absent an extension by Congress, enrollees will see significant premium hikes when open enrollment for 2026 begins Nov. 1. The ACA, known colloquially as Obamacare, also allows individuals to enroll in plans via a state-based marketplace rather than through an employer.

While Democrats in Congress have argued that the extensions are necessary to offset rising costs for lower-income Americans, the tax credits have become the latest target of broad cuts pushed by the Republican majority.

Due to the uncertainty, most managed care insurers have already filed rates with the government for materially higher premiums, according to Leerink analyst Whit Mayo.

Comparing it to the recent spate of Medicaid redeterminations — during which states decided who met qualifications for government-subsidized Medicaid health plans after years of COVID-related delays — Mayo said millions of people could either lose their health insurance or face much higher premiums.

"It's a very similar population [to Medicaid], the healthy people that probably could go without health insurance decide not to keep it, and then the sick people and those that need healthcare will retain it," Mayo said. "Therefore, you see a deterioration in your risk pool and more volatility in your cost trends."

Both the ACA credits and Medicare star ratings are expected to be discussed during the upcoming third-quarter managed care earnings season, which kicks off with Elevance on Oct. 21.

data.title: Mercer Trades For A Price Because Its Financial Risk Is Real And Near
data.description:
Mercer International (NASDAQ:MERC) is a wood pulp and lumber producer with large operations in Germany, Canada, and the US.

The company operates in relatively mature markets in pulp and lumber, where profitability moves cyclically with available capacity. The company does not participate in forestry.

Currently, the mill cycle is in a tight spot, with the company posting some of the lowest margins in its history. To this, we add a leveraged balance sheet, and the difficulty of improving it without substantial dilution, given where the stock price sits today.

The company's pricing seems attractive if we could expect a cyclical recovery in margins, but it is not exempt from large risks related to liquidity if margins do not recover relatively soon (within a year), or worse, if the global economy cools. I believe the name is a Hold for this reason, but could understand a speculative Buy position.

Pulp and lumber

Mercer's business is the processing of wood to transform it into many different outputs: pulps used for paper, lumber for construction and furniture (in different qualities and types), electricity, biofuels, and even chemicals.

The company has two segments, pulps and solid woods, each including the proceeds from the adjacent activities they generate (mainly biofuels and electricity from residues).

Pulps

The pulp segment operates in Germany and Canada, each with a capacity of approximately 1 million tons of pulp per year, divided into two mills each. It generates 70%+ of the company's revenues, and a corresponding amount of its EBITDA profitability.

Both Germany and Canada are some of the largest producers of timber of the Northern varieties (including also the US, Russia, and the Nordics). The market is also composed of southern varieties, where Brazil and China play a large role.

The company also concentrates on soft pulps. Soft pulps make up about 35/40% of the pulp market, and are blended with hard pulps, especially in some products like tissues, which make up more than 50% of the soft market (from the company's latest 10-K).

The company is not a large player in any of these markets (only 2.5% market share if we consider the smallest segmentation of soft and northern), being a commodity producer. However, it has claimed in its presentation as well as in a recent earnings call to have low-cost positions.

Lumber(s)

The company also manufactures lumber, mainly in two facilities in Germany. These are used for furniture and construction, with most of the company's revenues generated in Germany and the US.

Mercer also has two facilities in the US that specialize in what's called mass timber, or structural construction lumber. These products can be used to build large structures. Their use is increasing significantly (Mercer projects a 20%+ CAGR for this market in the US in its presentation) because it can replace (part of) the use of steel and cement, two industries considered 'hard to abate', or hard to bring to low levels of CO2 pollution.

Associated energy and subproducts

The company also produces energy for its own consumption and resale, plus chemicals and biofuels, from the different residues generated on its plants. These businesses are not very large in terms of revenue.

Cyclical industry

From a demand perspective, given the role of tissues and paper in demand (a consumable), I would believe the pulp market is not very cyclical, but rather mature, with growth in emerging markets (1/3 of demand for Mercer's pulps comes from China). Lumber, on the other hand, is a different thing, because demand can be more cyclical in all of its end markets (furniture and construction, above all, are very cyclical).

In turn, given that Mercer does not participate in foresting (it does not own plantations), it has to buy what it calls 'fibers', either timber or its residues. These, according to the 10-K, are 55% of the costs of pulp and 75% of the cost of lumber. This market has cyclicality in supply, given that there's a lag between demand signals and the expansion/contraction of supply (it takes time to grow a tree).

From the company's revenues and gross margins, however, what I believe is that the company's cycle is determined by its ability to pass its fiber costs downstream, which in turn is driven by aggregate demand and capacity for its products. We can observe below that the company's cycle in its asset return (second chart below, EBITDA over total assets) corresponds with periods where revenue has expanded or contracted more than CoGS (expanding/contracting gross margins).

When there's more demand than capacity, the company can earn higher processing or cost-plus margins. The contrary happens during lower demand periods, until supply leaves the market.

Current challenging cycle and financial position

Like many cyclical industries, wood processing is not doing well after the post-pandemic, given a deceleration of the global economy, and especially of China, plus overinvestment in capacity in many areas.

We can observe above that not only is the EBITDA margin on assets almost 50% below the recent historical average, but that it remains very close to historical minimums (with the exception potentially of the post-GFC).

The chart above does not reflect it, but current margins are similar to or even worse than those prevalent in 2023/24. The latter looks worse because of (non-recurring) impairments.

To this cyclical challenge, we have to add a tight financial position, which stems from overinvestment in the last leg of the cycle when it was positive. The company acquired mills with debt, and now faces high-interest charges.

After the refinancing of its Notes maturing in 2026, the company does not face large maturities until 2028, but had to swap low-yielding 2026 debt for 12% yielding 2028 debt ($400 million or 25% of its debt). In addition to this, it owes almost $900 million maturing in 2029 and yielding ~5%, and about $250 million in credit facilities and leases (see 2Q25 10-Q under Note 5 Debt).

This makes the company's position much more risky, because it cannot sustain its current operations for long without cash infusions. Currently, Mercer has about $150 million in cash available.

The company's EBITDA levels are around $175 million and decreasing (1H25 EBITDA was only $30 million). With these $175 million, it cannot cover maintenance CAPEX (considered $100 million in the latest call) and interest expenses of about $110 million.

If the company maintained its current TTM margins, it would still lose $35 million in cash per year, not too terrible. However, if margins decrease to 1H25 levels, then it will lose more than $100 million per year.

Mercer's management reassures that in 2H25 the cycle will improve, as some Canadian companies have closed operations (benefiting its Canadian pulp operations), and because European lumber is more competitive in the US, thanks to duties on Canadian production (see latest call). Management is also confident that liquidity will be fine because it still has access to those $150 million, plus EUR 175 million (~$200 million) and CAD 140 million (~$100 million) in revolving credit lines.

Valuation reflects large financing risks

Despite the availability of cash and credit facilities, if the situation does not improve quickly, Mercer will face financial challenges, and that is reflected in a deeply discounted price, if we compare it to what the company could earn on the basis of a cyclical return to the mean.

For example, based on an average EBIT margin over assets of 7.7% over the past 15 years, and an asset base of $2.4 billion, we could expect the median-cycle EBIT to be $185 million. Removing $110 million in interest and 25% taxes, that leaves us with about $56 million in net income, which is fairly high compared to a current market cap of about $175 million.

If we considered that the company could probably repay debts instead of investing in CAPEX for at least about $70 million ($170 million in D&A and $100 million in maintenance CAPEX), then a yearly deleveraging of about $100 million is possible, which would add another $12 million in savings on interest expenses (based on the most expensive debt).

However, in order to reach that cycle-average profitability, the company has to survive a downcycle of unknown length. Not only are we not sure the cycle will recover quickly, but we don't know if it could get worse, particularly for lumber (given the exposure to cyclical housing demand).

If the cycle were longer or more violent, then Mercer's current balance sheet would become a risk. In addition, because of the low market cap, any financing via equity would be hugely dilutive without a meaningful improvement in the balance sheet. For example, by diluting shareholders by 30% (issuing 50% of its current market cap) the company could potentially raise less than $85 million, which is less than a year of interest.

Mercer has indeed recently filed a shelf for $750 million in mixed securities (could be debt, common, or preferred equity). If the company reached a more sustainable or cheaper debt situation without significant dilution, then the expectations might change.

However, as things go now, I believe the risk of Mercer's equity being diluted is very high, and therefore believe that the name cannot qualify as a Buy. For this reason, I initiate with a Hold on Mercer, but understand that the stock might be interesting for a speculative play on a cyclical recovery, and that some readers might find it a good speculative Buy.

data.title: Cargojet: Moat With A Clear Growth Runway Is More Infrastructure Than Cyclical
data.description:
All figures are listed in $CAD unless otherwise noted.

All financial data is from Seeking Alpha unless otherwise noted.

Investment Thesis

We initiated coverage on Cargojet (TSX:CJT:CA) on March 2, 2025, with a 'Strong Buy' rating. Since then, the stock has delivered a disappointing –9.3% total return, well below the SPTSX's 19.5%. Cargojet had actually been outperforming the index until mid-August... but over the past month, shares have fallen nearly 15%, and are down 18.6% in the last two months. The selloff isn’t entirely without reason, but it’s clearly overdone.

We continue to believe the market is mispricing Cargojet’s fundamentals. The company isn’t as cyclical as it appears. Rather, it has built a defensible moat in Canada’s domestic overnight air freight market. Meanwhile, Canadian e-commerce remains underpenetrated relative to other developed markets, providing a runway for growth.

At today’s valuation, investors can own a best-in-class operator with strong cash generation, strategic partnerships, and durable competitive advantages at a discount to its historical multiples. We see this as an attractive long-term entry point for patient investors.

The North is Still Logging On

Cargojet operates Canada’s leading network for time-sensitive overnight air cargo, and a lot of that volume is driven by e-commerce activity. However, Canada’s e-commerce penetration rate remains significantly lower than that of other developed markets such as the United States.

As of July 2025, online retail accounted for 6.1% of total retail sales in Canada, which is roughly unchanged from prior months (6.0% in June, 6.1% in May, and 6.3% in April). By comparison, U.S. e-commerce sales represented 16.3% of all retail sales in Q2 2025 (up from 16.1% in Q1), highlighting Canada’s relative under-penetration and the long runway for growth in domestic e-commerce logistics demand.

We consider that there are structural differences in these economies that could drive this difference. Canada's smaller population and lower population density (~4.3 people per square km vs the U.S. at ~38) drives up shipping and fulfillment costs, which makes e-commerce penetration less viable.

That said, the roughly 10% gap between Canada’s and the U.S.’s e-commerce penetration is wider than expected. Even assuming a conservative scenario where Canada’s rate rises to 10% of total retail sales (+4% from current levels), the implications are meaningful. Between August 2024 and July 2025, Canadian retail sales totaled $828.45 billion, so a 4% shift to online channels would represent an additional $33.1 billion in goods requiring shipment.

As Canada’s largest provider of overnight air-cargo services, Cargojet stands to directly benefit from any sustained growth in e-commerce activity. In the first half of 2025, domestic network revenue rose 15.4% to $207.1 million, accounting for just under half of the company’s total revenue across its domestic network, ACMI, and charter segments. This growth highlights the strength of Cargojet’s position in the domestic logistics market, and there is still meaningful upside as Canada’s e-commerce penetration continues to expand.

Cyclical Industry Doesn't Always Equal Cyclical Company

Cargojet’s largest customers include Canada Post/Purolator, DHL, UPS Canada, Amazon, and Great Vision HK Express. Revenue concentration is high but is gradually improving. In 2024, the top 10 clients represented 75% of total revenue, down from 79% in 2023, while the top three clients accounted for 56%, compared to 59% in the prior year.

This concentration shows Cargojet’s deep integration with a small group of large-scale logistics players which provides both stability and exposure risk. To mitigate the latter and strengthen incentives/alignment, Cargojet has structured stock warrant agreements with two of its largest partners, Amazon and DHL.

Amazon holds the right to acquire up to 15.9% of Cargojet’s outstanding shares at preset strike prices (10.6% at $91.78/share and 5.3% at $186.57/share), while DHL may acquire up to 6.6% at $93.61/share, contingent on achieving specific revenue thresholds (e.g. $3.2 billion in revenue over eight years for DHL). These incentives effectively tie long-term business volumes to Cargojet’s enterprise value, giving customers “skin in the game". More importantly, both agreements were extended in mid-2025. Amazon’s contract (renewed July 2, 2025) for four years plus a two-year renewal option, and DHL’s (renewed August 6, 2025) under similar terms.

Transportation is known to be a cyclical industry, and the above background is meant to provide context as to why Cargojet is not a cyclical as it may seem.

Cargojet itself acknowledges that most of its contracts are for a term of 3 to 10 years, and the new contracts with DHL and Amazon reduce some of the contract risk that was inherent a year ago. It's for this reason that Cargojet can, and in some cases should, be viewed as more of an infrastructure-type business. Long-term contracts with price hikes are Cargojet's bread and butter and there is potential to outperform (and underperform) with the ACMI and all-in charter business.

Cargojet's 2-year beta of 1.57 points to a cyclical company, and compared to similar U.S. counterpart companies such as UPS (0.89), FDX (0.97), and EXPD (0.7), which are just as cyclical, drawdowns like the one we've seen with Cargojet over the past 2 months feel overblown.

Cargojet is Flying in a League of Its Own

Cargojet doesn't have any significant competition in the main portion of the business because of the Canada-US Open Skies agreement.

The current Canada — US “Open Skies” agreement provides regulation of the airline industry, including the air cargo industry, within Canada and currently provides protection of domestic national carriers in each country. The agreement allows cross-border flights between Canada and the United States but provides major restrictions on carriers from operating flight routes between two points within the other’s country. The most recent amendments negotiated between the two countries reinforced the restriction of cabotage and does not allow United States carriers to establish domestic flight routes within Canada and Canadian carriers including Cargojet to establish domestic routes within the United States.

While this is one part of the reason why Cargojet doesn't have a lot of real competition, other factors, such as long-term customer contracts, high switching costs, the cost to build a network and fleet to a similar scale as Cargojet, and the difficulty of replicating the operational expertise, all position Cargojet in a monopoly-type situation.

Positives Beyond The Thesis

With the recent pullback in share price, Cargojet now trades at a forward P/E and forward EV/EBITDA ratio that is well below the 3-year historical average. In conjunction with our original thesis, the current share price marks an excellent entry point for a company with this type of moat and potential growth.

Cargojet's growth has consistently delivered. The dividend has grown at an 8.4% CAGR over the past five years while at the same time reducing the share count from 17.3 million in Q4 2021 to 15.1 million as of Q2 2025 through share buybacks. Over the past decade, EBITDA has expanded at a 27% annual rate. In the last year, revenue increased 14.4% and gross profit margin improved to 23.2%, up 1.3% relative to the five-year average.

Revenue and operating income growth continued to be supported by a 7.9% return on invested capital over the trailing twelve months and $150.6 million spent on growth capital expenditures during the same period.

Cargojet’s free cash flow generation is where shareholder's focus should be, and while reported free cash flow was -$103.9 million over the past year, this includes growth capex. On a maintenance-capex only basis, the business generated $46.7 million, or $3.10 per share, in adjusted free cash flow, yielding an adjusted FCF yield of 3.7%.

Given the durable moat, improving margins, and significant runway for growth, Cargojet is undervalued relative to its earnings and cash flow potential. The price target for Cargojet with an FCF yield of 3% is $103.33/share, representing an upside of 23%.

Key Risks

Cargojet faces several risks that could affect its operations and valuation. It's important to note that this list isn't exhaustive, and other risks could materially impact the company's long-term outlook.

First, regulatory changes in the Canadian aviation and air cargo sectors could impact the company’s business. This risk is somewhat mitigated by the high cost and complexity of replicating Cargojet’s network, as well as recent protectionist tendencies in both the U.S. and Canada that limit foreign competition on domestic routes.

Second, while ongoing trade tensions and tariffs between Canada and other countries could affect cross-border trade, Cargojet’s domestic-focused network reduces its exposure relative to international carriers. There is also the potential for this risk to be less impactful if the next U.S. president doesn't take the same stance on trade.

Third, the company’s long-term growth is likely closely tied to Canadian retail sales trends. Slower than expected retail growth or structural changes in consumer behavior could dampen Cargojet’s revenue expansion, especially if e-commerce market penetration doesn't materialize in a meaningful way.

Finally, as with any air cargo operator, there is the risk of a material incident, such as a plane crash. Such an event could harm the company’s reputation, disrupt operations, and result in significant financial losses.

Conclusion

Cargojet represents a compelling opportunity for long-term investors. The company has built a defensible position in Canada’s domestic overnight air cargo market, benefiting from structural barriers to entry, long-term customer contracts, and high switching costs. With Canada’s e-commerce market still underpenetrated relative to other developed economies, Cargojet has a significant runway for revenue and cash flow growth.

Financially, the company has delivered consistent growth in EBITDA, dividends, and adjusted free cash flow, while maintaining disciplined capital allocation through buybacks and growth capex. With the recent share price pullback, Cargojet trades below its historical multiples, offering investors exposure to a high-quality operator at an attractive entry point.

While risks are inherent with the investment, including things like regulatory changes, trade tensions, dependence on Canadian retail trends, and operational incidents, the company’s scale, operational expertise, and strategic partnerships mitigate some of these concerns. On balance, Cargojet’s combination of growth potential, cash generation, and durable competitive advantages supports our view that the stock is undervalued and well-positioned for long-term returns.

data.title: Dividend Investing: Thinking Beyond The Vanilla Approach
data.description:
By Karen Watkin, CFA & Edward Williams

Multi-asset investors should consider a deep bench of factors besides dividends in the hunt for income.

Dividends remain a key income source for multi-asset investors. But we think there are more ingredients to think about when designing an approach to capture equity income. Yield is important, of course, but other factors may help investors balance income with broader return potential.

A Narrow Focus on Income Alone Has Its Trade-Offs

Over the last three decades, dividend stocks have delivered total returns similar to those of the broad global equity market (Display, left). But equity dividend performance has been episodic, with a high degree of variability versus the broader market (Display, right).

As we see it, focusing purely on dividend yield can create another problem: narrowing your investment universe to only high-dividend payers can drive unintentional biases in exposures to market segments. For example, high-dividend indices tend to lean into Europe and defensive sectors such as utilities and consumer staples. Conversely, they’re often underweight the US and technology—the very areas that have driven much of the market’s growth over the past decade (Display).

In our view, this creates a significant opportunity cost. Many of today’s most innovative and profitable companies—particularly in US tech—reinvest earnings rather than pay dividends. A glimpse at the five largest stocks in global and high-dividend equity benchmarks highlights this point (Display).

Companies such as NVIDIA have seen strong upside in recent years but pay little to nothing in dividends. By excluding these growth leaders, traditional dividend strategies risk missing out on key sources of capital appreciation and future growth. We think this trend is likely to continue, given the rise of artificial intelligence.

High Dividend Stocks May Omit Growth Leaders

A More Holistic Way to Capture Dividend Income

We think a diversified, systematic approach that reaches beyond traditional yield sources to pursue long-term growth potential can be especially effective. By using a quantitative framework, we can tilt toward companies that score well across a broader set of factors, especially:

  • Quality—firms with strong balance sheets and stable earnings
  • Momentum—companies with positive price trends amid ongoing rallies
  • Low Volatility—steady names that add a defensive layer when markets dip
  • Value—business models attractively priced relative to strong fundamentals and prospects

We believe this type of multifactor income produces a more balanced strategy with the potential to deliver competitive income while maintaining growth potential and staying agile across market regimes. The patterns of April’s sharp market downturn and recovery showcased the complementary nature: low-volatility and value stocks offered downside mitigation, while quality, momentum and growth-oriented stocks led the rebound.

Taking Advantage of Regional Market Divergence

Another advantage of a systematic approach is its flexibility to tailor exposures by region. Equity markets vary significantly across geographies, and aligning factor tilts with local strengths can enhance outcomes.

For example, dividend yields tend to be higher—and value opportunities more abundant—in Europe, especially in sectors like financials. The region’s banks and insurers have benefited from rising interest rates, which have boosted net interest margins and improved profitability. These “value compounders” have delivered strong returns in recent years, making Europe a fertile ground for dividend and value tilts.

In contrast, US markets are dominated by growth and innovation, with tech firms offering modest dividends but exceptional earnings growth. In this case, leaning into quality, growth and momentum factors may help capture upside potential alongside higher income-generating assets.

To sum things up, plain-vanilla dividend strategies may offer the income that investors seek, but it’s often at the expense of diversification or exposure to upside potential. A systematic, multifactor approach offers access to more flavors in a balanced strategy that we believe may be better equipped to navigate changing market conditions.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

References to specific securities discussed are for illustrative purposes only and should not to be considered recommendations by AllianceBernstein L.P. It should not be assumed that investments in the securities mentioned have necessarily been or will necessarily be profitable.

data.title: PayPal's Growth And Buybacks Are Expected To Double Shareholders Returns
data.description:
Investment Thesis

PayPal Holdings Inc. (NASDAQ:PYPL) presents a highly attractive investment opportunity. Its current P/E ratio of slightly under 15 underscores how the market continues to undervalue its long-term cash flow potential. Additionally, growth in net income, expansion of the PayPal world to facilitate global trade, and strategic partnerships with Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) and TerraPay are strengthening its ecosystem and total addressable market.

PayPal's $7 billion sale of its U.S. Buy Now Pay Later [BNPL] receivables to Blue Owl Capital is another major catalyst marking its transition to a “light balance sheet.” It mitigates credit risk while preserving customer relationships. With a strong free cash flow exceeding $5 billion annually and current 2025 TTM ROIC of 21.8%, ROE of 23%, and ROA of 5.7%, PayPal demonstrates efficient capital deployment.

The company is trading at $67 per share, while its fair value based on its free cash flow stands at $143 per share at a discount rate of 8%, offering more than 100% upside supported by multiple catalysts driving its revaluation and price. PayPal’s $15 billion share repurchase authorization, following a $6 billion buyback in 2024, indicates a significant reduction in outstanding shares by over 2 billion in the next few years could increase its earnings per share and double shareholder value.

Company Overview

PayPal operates as a global two-sided payments platform. It helps connect consumers and merchants across 200 markets with over 434 million active accounts.

PayPal generates its revenues mainly through transaction fees, crypto services, currency conversion, instant transfer, value added offerings such as credit products, partnerships, and subscriptions.

Business Model

PayPal operates a platform-based, transaction-driven business model strengthened by convenient digital transactions. It provides digital wallets through PayPal and Venmo, supporting payment through bank accounts, reward cards, balances, and cryptocurrencies. In addition, it also provides peer-to-peer transfers, buy now pay later, and access to credit products. For merchants, it delivers online and in-person payment solutions such as PayPal checkout, payout services, Zettle and Braintree. Merchants also benefit from financing options, global customer reach, and management tools.

The company generates revenue through transaction fees, which represent about 91% of its total revenue. Other services like value-added services, including consumer and merchant credit products, partnerships, referral fees, and interest on customer balances, also contribute to its revenue.

PayPal’s cost structure is driven by payment processing, regulatory requirements, and compliance, as well as ongoing investments in technology, sales, and marketing.

Business Segments

PayPal reports its operations under two main segments:

Transaction revenue: Revenue generated from fees charged to consumers and merchants for payment transactions across PayPal, Braintree, Venmo, Zettie, PayPal Pay Later, PayPal Complete Payments, and cryptocurrency services. This segment generated $28.84 billion in 2024 and $7.4 billion in Q2 2025, indicating 89.8% of net revenue with a 4% y-o-y increase.

Value-added services: This segment includes revenue from referral fees, gateway services, partnerships, interest, and fees on consumer as well as merchant credit products like PayPal Business and PayPal Working Capital, and Buy Now Pay Later. In addition to this, the revenue is also generated through interest income earned on customer balances. Revenue generated from this segment in 2024 was $2.95 billion and $847 million in Q2 2025.

PayPal’s Strength

1. Positive And Growing Free Cash Flows

PayPal has consistently generated positive free cash flows, growing more than 10% over the past 3 years due to its unique business model that requires lower capital to sustain its business and generate higher margins. PayPal is highly trusted, and its recognized payment option is what drives customers as well as merchants' confidence.

2. Share Buybacks

PayPal has been repurchasing shares for the last 5 years, leveraging its strong free cash flows to return excess capital to its shareholders. PayPal has spent more than $30 billion since 2015 to buy back shares.

In 2024, the company repurchased 92 million shares, spending approximately $6 billion as part of the company’s surplus capital return strategy. The goal was to enhance shareholder value as well as reduce the number of shares outstanding. The board authorized an additional $15 billion stock repurchase program with no expiration date in 2024.

It reduced approximately 16% of its outstanding shares from 1.10 billion in 2023 to approximately 977 million in 2025, thus creating significant shareholder value.

3. Platform Scale And Strategic Partnership With Google

With 434 million active accounts, PayPal can leverage its transactional data to personalize experiences by identifying the trends and unlocking new opportunities to generate more revenue. Its strategic partnership with Google is to enhance e-commerce and payment. Therefore, its collaboration with Google and other tech companies, merchants, and payment networks is to expand the company’s reach and reinforce its role around the global payment ecosystem.

PayPal also committed to investing $100 million to accelerate digital payment solutions for businesses as well as partnering with TerraPay to enable real-time fund transfer across Africa and the Middle East.

Weaknesses

1. Overreliance On Branded Checkouts, Growing Debt, And Cost Cuts

The company’s checkout button is its core profit driver, which generates nearly 80% of its profits. Big players like Apple Pay, as well as Google Pay, are growing on mobile, and merchants are selecting cheaper options. Since profits are so concentrated in checkouts, a small market share loss can significantly hit its earnings.

In addition, historically the company has been maintaining a low debt level, relying on its strong free cash flows to fund its operations. However, in recent years, the company’s debt has been increasing, which would reduce its free cash flows if interest rates remain high.

Also in January 2024, the company announced it would cut 2,500 jobs, which were spread across departments, targeting overlapping functions and cutting down on less profitable initiatives. This decision was part of the company’s strategic effort to “right size,” and the goal was to streamline operations, reduce duplication, complexity to enhance efficiency.

Financial Analysis

Growing Revenue & Net Income (2025 TTM)

In the last 3 years, the company’s revenue grew from $29.77 billion in 2023 to $32.29 billion in 2025 [TTM] indicating an increase of 8%. This is a result of the company successfully expanding its user base and monetizing it. However, PayPal now is facing the challenge of maintaining and sustaining this growth due to stiff competition from other platforms like Stripe and Wise plc (OTCPK:WPLCF).

Despite these challenges, PayPal’s net income has indicated a strong upward trajectory, which nearly doubled since 2022, increasing to $4.67 billion in 2025 [TTM] at a CAGR of 17.93%. However, its net income has been more volatile, despite growing year over year on average with strong margin expansion close to 14%.

Since 2023, the company, despite earlier losses, managed to achieve $383 million in net gains on its strategic investments and interest income.

In the last 3 years, its performance improved mainly due to growth in revenue, operating efficiently, and improving the performance of its strategic investments that boosted its net income. Moreover, PayPal’s increasing debt to $11.30 billion in 2025 from $9.6 billion has negatively affected its free cash flow. Its FCF to debt dropped below 50% in 2025.

PayPal FCF has decreased from $6.76 billion in 2024 to $5.29 billion in 2025 [TTM].

FCF-Based Valuation

Assumptions Reason
Revenue Growth Expected to grow at 12% vs 14% historical avg. growth in the next 5 years
FCF Growth Expected to grow at 10% vs historical avg. growth of 15% in the next 5 years
Earnings Growth Expected to grow at 14% based on its historical avg. growth in the next 5 years
Discount Rate (Opportunity Cost) 8% based on the Fed's rate cut in the last meeting
Outstanding Shares (Diluted) 1.039M (2024). Diluted outstanding shares reduced to 977M in 2025 [TTM]
Perpetuity Growth Rate 3% (expected rate of inflation in the medium to long term)
Period 5 Yrs. Medium term

Estimated Fair Value

Based on PayPal’s FCF growth for the last 5 years, I expect the sum of its free cash flows to grow to $27 billion after discounting at 8%. Therefore, the company seems to be undervalued at its current market price of $66.89. Its fair value should be close to $143 per share based on my FCF based intrinsic value calculation. As support for my thesis, the company is already making significant changes, such as ongoing product diversification by offering expanded crypto features, which is expected to boost its revenues. PayPal World has strengthened its core business for profitability, which underpins the management’s efforts to maximize long-term value for its shareholders.

Catalysts Driving PayPal's Value

PayPal’s performance has been strong in Q2 2025, which signals an operational efficiency and potential for the stock to grow close to its fair value in the near future. The company’s Q2 report highlights improved profitability, supported by an expanding total addressable market, strategic product innovation, and share repurchases.

Based on PayPal’s share buyback program, approximately 2.14 billion shares are expected to be reduced over the next 3-4 years. The reduction in outstanding shares acts as a major catalyst for uplifting the company’s stock price, let alone revenue and FCF growth, which are also expected to grow above 11% based on its last 3 years trend.

Therefore, based on its current run rate for its share buybacks, PayPal’s current stock price could potentially multiply by nearly 7 times, creating shareholder value.

PayPal had a strong momentum for its Venmo platform, where double-digit revenue growth continues as the company expands its checkout points, merchant partnerships, card usage. Its buy now and pay later volumes also accelerated, and active account trends also significantly improved, with the company beginning to re-engage with dormant users, thus creating an opportunity to deepen cross-selling across its ecosystem.

PayPal announced the launch of PayPal World Platform that aims to facilitate global trade. Through this platform, the customers will be able to use their local wallets and payment methods to make payments to each other. PayPal now is investing heavily in artificial intelligence to improve streamlining checkout processes and to improve fraud detection and also to personalize its user experience.

PayPal’s $7 Billion Sale Of Its U.S. Buy Now Pay Later Receivables To Blue Owl Capital Acts As A Catalyst

This deal allows the company to transfer credit risk off its balance sheet while continuing to handle underwriting and servicing, meaning PayPal can still keep control over its customer relationships and the Buy Now Pay Later portfolio. This move indicates the company’s shift towards a “light balance sheet model,” reducing its exposure to potential loan defaults and improving efficiency, which was affecting its profitability.

Other Key Valuation Metrics Supporting PayPal’s Fair Value

PayPal key valuation metrics indicate strong underlying profitability and operational efficiency that support our thesis that PayPal’s current market value will move towards its fair value in the short to medium term. The company’s ROIC of 21.8% in 2025 [TTM] indicates a highly efficient capital allocation strategy by the company’s management using capital effectively.

In addition, its current ROE of 23% and ROA of 5.7% this year in 2025 (TTM) suggest above-average returns relative to its competitors from the financial industry. These returns highlight PayPal’s effective management policy of consistently generating above-average returns by keeping its assets and equity levels lower to mitigate risk and return excess capital to its rightful owners, i.e., its shareholders, through share buybacks.

PayPal’s current P/E ratio of under 15 remains well below the market and peer valuation, indicating PayPal is deeply undervalued.

Key Challenges And Risks

PayPal faces high competition from fintech giants like Apple (AAPL), Google Pay, and Stripe and other emerging players in Buy Now Pay Later companies like Affirm. Earlier in 2001, the company had problems with online fraud as a result of international hackers targeting the company’s accounts and transferring small amounts of money out of multiple accounts. But the company has since then developed a monitoring system to detect fraudulent transactions.

However, despite these efforts, in 2015 around 150,000 Spanish cardholders faced their funds being frozen by regulators, apparently affecting PayPal customers.

PayPal Penalties And Regulatory Settlements

Date

Regulatory Body

Violation Description

Penalty Amount

2020

OFAC U.S. Treasury

Transactions (2009-2013) involving sanctioned entities in Cuba, Iran, Sudan, and WMD proliferators due to inadequate screening filters

$7.7 million Settlement

2025

NYDFS

Cybersecurity failure exposing approximately 35,000 users’ SSNs via unsecured IRS Form 1099-K access.

$2 million fine

The company has been regularly hit by fines for breach of sanctions to carry out transactions involving sanctioned countries and entities in Iran, Sudan, Cuba, and other groups linked to weapons of mass destruction. PayPal’s screening filters were inadequate, and its compliance systems failed to block such transactions. And the company had to pay $7.7 million as a settlement fee for such violations in 2020.

There was another cybersecurity lapse involving exposure to the social security number of about 35,000 users. This breach occurred due to unsecured access to IRD form 1099-K data. The company had to pay a $2 million fine as recently as 2025.

Therefore, despite investing in technology and creating detective tools, PayPal still runs the risk of data breaches. In addition, changes in data privacy laws and financial regulations can negatively impact the company’s operations unless it has stronger controls.

Technical glitches can disrupt services, which can lead to user dissatisfaction and loss of revenue. PayPal also faces several market-related risks and is exposed to interest rate risk due to current changes in interest rates being lowered, affecting its income earned on its short-term investments such as Treasuries and bond portfolios. Adverse movement in interest rates reduces customer spending and also increases delinquencies on PayPal's credit products.

Besides this, currency markets have also been very volatile recently due to trade wars between the US, China, and the EU, and exchange rate movements have also put pressure on its money transfer loss fee.

PayPal is heavily dependent on its revenue from foreign exchange rates due to its large international operations in the UK, Europe, and Australia. Therefore, adverse movement in exchange rates for these countries has a huge impact on PayPal’s earnings and its cash flows.

In order to mitigate these risks, the company uses an effective hedging strategy to deal with volatility in exchange rates, but it could affect its financials to some extent, which in turn could possibly incur minor losses for its business.

The company also made strategic investments close to $1.6 billion in 2025 in some public and private companies and incurred a significant loss, which has affected its profitability and may continue for a while unless it decides to exit some of these non-profitable minority positions.

Conclusion

PayPal offers an attractive investment opportunity for its shareholders and investors. Despite significant operational risks and regulatory breaches and fines, which have resulted in its share price losing value, the company has the potential to recover and reach its estimated fair value of $143 in the short to medium term. Its free cash flow strength, omnichannel presence, and ability to innovate across payments and financial services provide a solid foundation for long-term growth despite all the challenges and stiff competition in the payment industry. While risks from increasing levels of its debt remain a genuine concern, it still has the ability to scale due to its brand trust and generate and grow its FCF.

Moreover, the historical performance suggests that it consistently produces profits and can withstand any external pressure. Therefore, it currently trades at a significant discount to its fair value, and the company presents a compelling risk-reward opportunity to investors seeking exposure to the digital payment sector who wish to invest medium to long-term to double their investment in the next 3-5 years.
